title-group.article-title: Clinicopathological characteristics and outcomes of gastric-type adenocarcinoma of the cervix: a systematic review

contrib-group.aff: 1From the Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology (Tjokroprawiro, Sulistya, Novitasari, Ulhaq), Faculty of Medicine, Universitas Airlangga, Surabaya, Indonesia
2Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology (Tjokroprawiro, Sulistya, Novitasari, Ulhaq), Dr. Soetomo General Academic Hospital, Surabaya, Indonesia
3Center for Global Surgery (Sulistya), ARC Institute, Surabaya, Indonesia
4Faculty of Medicine (Wijaya), Universitas Sumatera Utara, Medan, Indonesia
5Faculty of Medicine (Rahma), Universitas Sebelas Maret, Surakarta, Indonesia
6Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Faculty of Medicine (Waluyo), Universitas Lambung Mangkurat, Ulin General Hospital, Banjarmasin, Indonesia
7Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology (Wang), Taipei Veterans General Hospital, Taipei, Taiwan
8Institute of Clinical Medicine (Wang), National Yang Ming Chiao Tung University, Taipei, Taiwan
10Department of Medical Research (Wang), China Medical University Hospital, Taichung, Taiwan

author-notes.corresp: ⁎Corresponding author: Brahmana Askandar Tjokroprawiro, MD, PhD. brahmanaaskandar@fk.unair.ac.id

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/).

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: We aimed to synthesize current evidence on the clinicopathological features, diagnostic strategies, treatment modalities, and outcomes of gastric-type adenocarcinoma (GAS) of the cervix, a rare and aggressive HPV-independent subtype of cervical adenocarcinoma.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: A systematic review was conducted in accordance with PRISMA 2020 guidelines and registered on PROSPERO. Comprehensive searches of PubMed, Scopus, ScienceDirect, and Springer were performed and supplemented by manual searches. Eligible studies were English-language case reports or case series describing histologically confirmed GAS, minimal deviation adenocarcinoma, or adenoma malignum. Data were extracted on clinicopathology, diagnostic modalities, treatments, and outcomes. Quality was appraised using the Joanna Briggs Institute checklists.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Fifty-nine studies were included, comprising 51 case reports and 8 case series, with a total of 150 patients. The mean age at diagnosis was 52.8 years, most commonly between 41 and 60 years. Vaginal bleeding and discharge were the leading initial symptoms, although over one-third lacked symptom data. Most cases were diagnosed at FIGO stage I-III, while 16% presented with stage IV disease. Radical hysterectomy was the predominant surgical approach, and concomitant chemoradiotherapy was the most frequent non-surgical treatment. IHC consistently demonstrated MUC6 and CK7 positivity, with frequent abnormal p53 and variable p16 expression. Recurrence occurred in 28.7% of patients, predominantly at distant sites, and 5-year survival was only 26.7%.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: GAS is a rare, distinct, and highly aggressive cervical adenocarcinoma with poor long-term outcomes and limited responsiveness to standard therapy. Improved diagnostic strategies and tailored treatment protocols are urgently needed.

def.p: Large Loop Excision of the Transformation Zone
Pelvic lymph node dissection/ Pelvic lymphadenectomy

caption.title: AJOG Global Reports at a Glance

sec.title: Why was this study conducted?
sec.p: Gastric-type adenocarcinoma (GAS) of the cervix is a rare, human papillomavirus-independent malignancy with distinctive morphology, aggressive behavior, and poor response to standard therapy. Owing to its rarity and frequent misdiagnosis, the current understanding of its clinicopathological characteristics and outcomes remains fragmented. This systematic review was conducted to consolidate existing evidence, describe global clinicopathological patterns, and identify diagnostic and therapeutic challenges associated with this uncommon subtype.

sec.title: Key findings?
sec.p: Fifty-nine studies comprising 150 patients were included. The mean age at diagnosis was 52.8 years, with vaginal bleeding and discharge as the most common symptoms. Most patients presented at FIGO stages I-III, while 16.0% had stage IV disease. Radical hysterectomy was the predominant surgical approach, and concurrent chemoradiotherapy was the most frequent nonsurgical treatment. Immunohistochemistry consistently showed MUC6 and CK7 positivity, frequent p53 abnormalities, and variable p16 expression, supporting HPV-independence. Recurrence occurred in 28.7% of patients, predominantly at distant sites, with a 5-year survival rate of only 26.7%.

sec.title: What does this study add to what is already known?
sec.p: This review provides the most comprehensive synthesis to date of GAS of the cervix, emphasizing its unique clinicopathological and molecular features distinct from HPV-related adenocarcinomas. It highlights the limitations of conventional screening, the importance of immunohistochemical markers for accurate diagnosis, and the urgent need for tailored treatment strategies and multicenter studies to improve outcomes in this rare, aggressive malignancy.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Cervical cancer (CC) is a major global health problem, with most cases caused by persistent high-risk HPV infection.1 While the majority are squamous carcinomas, adenocarcinomas form a smaller proportion. Almost all are HPV-related, yet a minority develop through HPV-independent pathways.2 Among these, gastric-type adenocarcinoma (GAS) is a rare but clinically important subtype with distinct morphology and aggressive behavior that histologically resembles gastric adenocarcinoma and is typically independent of human papillomavirus (HPV) infection, a key differentiator from the usual-type endocervical adenocarcinoma.3,4

The 2020 WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumors recognizes GAS as a separate entity, emphasizing the importance of its distinction via histopathological and immunohistochemical (IHC) analysis in its diagnosis.5 Unlike HPV-related tumors, GAS arises through an alternative molecular pathway and presents unique diagnostic and therapeutic challenges. Although recognition of GAS has improved in recent years, the condition remains rare, and much of the current evidence is limited to small case series and retrospective cohorts.6,7 Available evidence suggests significant heterogeneity in clinical presentation, response to therapy, and outcomes, raising questions about the applicability of traditional treatment protocols designed for HPV-positive CCs.4

Despite growing recognition, the true global burden of GAS remains unclear, as many cases are underdiagnosed or misclassified due to overlap with minimal deviation adenocarcinoma (MDA) and other mucinous subtypes, particularly in regions with limited access to IHC. Conventional cervical screening methods, including HPV testing and Pap smears, show poor sensitivity for detecting GAS, likely due to its nature as HPV-independent and bland cytologic features and frequent localization in the upper endocervical canal.8, 9, 10

Given these diagnostic challenges, prognostic uncertainties, and the scarcity of large-scale studies, current understanding of GAS remains fragmented. To address this gap, we conducted a systematic review of published cases to synthesize clinical characteristics, pathological features, and outcomes of GAS of the cervix. This work aims to provide a comprehensive overview of existing evidence, highlight recurrent diagnostic pitfalls, and identify areas where further research is urgently needed.

sec.title: Systematic search
sec.p: This systematic review was performed in accordance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) 2020 guidelines11 and registered on PROSPERO (CRD420251150519). The detailed PICOS framework are provided in Supplementary Table 1. We sought to answer the research question: What are the clinicopathological features, diagnostic strategies, treatment approaches, and patient outcomes of gastric-type adenocarcinoma of the cervix reported in the literature?

sec.title: Search strategy
sec.p: A systematic search was performed on September 1, 2025, in PubMed, ScienceDirect, Scopus, and Springer. Additional relevant articles were identified through manual searching. Keywords included “gastric-type adenocarcinoma,” “cervical adenocarcinoma,” “HPV-independent,” and “gastric phenotype,” with no restrictions on date or language. Detailed search strings for each database, including MeSH terms and Boolean operators, are provided in Supplementary Table 2. Eligible studies were case reports or case series, involving adult female patients with histologically confirmed GAS, MDA, or adenoma malignum of the cervix. Exclusion criteria were inaccessible articles, studies without relevant or valid data, and reviews, abstracts, or correspondences.

sec.title: Data extraction and synthesis
sec.p: In this review, data extraction was conducted systematically from each included case report and case series. The following variables were collected: author and year of publication, country of study, cancer stage when available, patient age, and initial clinical presentation. Histopathological findings were consistently recorded, along with IHC markers and molecular or biomarker data where these were reported. Additional information included comorbidities, and imaging modalities employed for diagnosis. Details of treatment were captured, including both initial and adjuvant therapy, as well as therapeutic strategies for recurrence when described. Follow-up information and the site of recurrence were also extracted when available. All reported frequencies and percentages were calculated based on the number of patients with available data for each variable, and denominators were adjusted accordingly to account for missing or unreported information. This structured approach enabled comprehensive synthesis of patient characteristics, diagnostic modalities, treatment patterns, and outcomes across the included studies.

sec.title: Quality assessment
sec.p: We independently assessed the risk of bias of the included studies using the Joanna Briggs Institute (JBI) critical appraisal checklists for case reports and case series.12,13 Following JBI guidance, results were presented as itemized checklists rather than as an accumulated score. For this review, however, we additionally categorized study quality: studies with ≥80% “Yes” responses were considered high quality, those with 70% to 79% as moderate quality, and those with ≤70% as low quality. Studies were further classified as case series or case reports according to a predefined criteria by.14 Data extraction was carried out by two independent authors (HAS and NW), whereas bias assessments were performed by another pair of independent authors (AAR and RAU). Any discrepancies were addressed by a third author (KN) in an independent manner.Statistical analysis

Meta-analysis could not be performed due to the rarity of gastric type endocervical adenocarcinoma, as the current systematic review is based entirely on published individual case reports and small case series. However, extracted data from eligible studies were compiled in a structured sheet and quantitatively assessed using descriptive statistics. Variables such as clinicopathologic presentation, staging, imaging, pathology, treatment modalities, IHC, molecular biomarker expression, surgical interventions, recurrence patterns, and survival outcomes were grouped and analyzed to evaluate their frequency and trends across reported cases.

sec.title: Study selection
sec.p:
The search results in 3094 records, of which 2825 were excluded due to duplicates (n=104) and (n=2721) marked ineligible by automation tools. After further screening and full-text assessment, a total of 59 published articles were included in this systematic review, of which 51 were case reports and 8 were case series PRISMA flow diagram (Figure 1) presents the process of study selection and the reasons for exclusion.Figure 1
PRISMA flow of study selection.Figure 1 dummy alt textTjokroprawiro. Clinicopathological characteristics and outcomes of gastric-type adenocarcinoma of the cervix. AJOG Glob Rep 2026.

sec.title: Quality assessment
sec.p: The methodological quality of the included studies was evaluated using the JBI critical appraisal checklist, with detailed scoring provided in (Supplementary Table 3). Based on the predefined categorization outlined in the Methods section, 39 studies were rated as high quality, 14 as moderate quality, and 6 as low quality.

sec.title: Study characteristics
sec.p:
A total of 59 studies met the inclusion criteria (Table 1). Most originated from Asia (72.9%), followed by North America 15.3%), Europe (8.5%), Australia (1.7%), and South America (1.7%). At the country level, the largest number of studies were from China (40.7%), with additional contributions from the United States (13.6%), South Korea (13.6%), Japan (10.1%), Spain (3.39%), and Singapore (3.3%), while nine studies (15.2%) originated from other countries. Regarding publication year, the majority were published between 2025, 2024, 2023, 2019, with the highest number in 2024 (22.0%). Seven studies each were published in 2025, 2023, and 2019 (11.9% each), followed by 2022 (10.2%), 2021 (10.2%), and 2020, 2017, and 2015 (5.0% each). Earlier reports between 2014, 2016, 2018 were less frequent, accounting for fewer than four studies per year. Most included reports had small sample sizes. Fifty studies (86.4%) described fewer than five cases, whereas only eight studies (13.6%) reported series with five or more patients.Table 1

≥58 (13.6)Tjokroprawiro. Clinicopathological characteristics and outcomes of gastric-type adenocarcinoma of the cervix. AJOG Glob Rep 2026.

A total of 59 studies included 150 individual patients (Table 2). The mean age at diagnosis was 52.8 years (SD ± 11.5), with one case (0.7%) lacking specific age information. Most patients were diagnosed between 41 and 60 years (60%), with fewer cases in younger (21–40 years, 14%) and older age groups (≥61 years, 26%). The most common initial presentations were vaginal bleeding (23.3%) and vaginal discharge (18.7%), followed by abnormal Pap smear findings (12.0%), pelvic or abdominal pain (4.7%), and asymptomatic or incidental detection (4.0%). More than one third of cases (37.3%) did not provide details on initial presentation.Table 2

Missing data of 0.7%; CT, Computed tomography; FIGO, International Federation of Gynecology and Obstetrics; HPV, Human papillomavirus; LEGH, Lobular endocervical glandular hyperplasia; MRI, Magnetic resonance imaging; PET-CT, Positron emission tomography-computed tomography; PJS, Peutz-Jeghers syndrome; SD, Standard deviation; TVUSG, Transvaginal ultrasonography; USG, Ultrasonography.Tjokroprawiro. Clinicopathological characteristics and outcomes of gastric-type adenocarcinoma of the cervix. AJOG Glob Rep 2026.

At diagnosis, patients were most frequently staged as FIGO I (22.7%), II (22%), or III (18.7%), while 16% presented at stage IV. A smaller proportion had carcinoma in situ (1.3%), and staging was not reported in 19.3% of cases. Comorbidity data were seldom provided. Diabetes mellitus type II (1.3%), hypertension (2.7%), and Peutz-Jeghers syndrome (PJS) (6.0%) were the most frequently reported, although 86.7% of patients had no comorbidity information available. Histopathological confirmation showed invasive gastric-type adenocarcinoma in 89.3% of patients, HPV-independent gastric-type adenocarcinoma in 8%, and lobular endocervical glandular hyperplasia in 2.7%.

Imaging was variably reported. Over half of the patients (57.3%) had no imaging data available. Among those with imaging, MRI was most frequently used as a single modality (10.0%), followed by CT (2.7%) and ultrasound (2.0%). Dual-modality approaches included CT plus MRI (4.0%) and combinations involving PET-CT, MRI, ultrasound, or TVUSG. Triple-modality imaging was reported in 8.2% of cases, most commonly USG plus CT plus MRI. Multiple-modality protocols involving four or more techniques were rare (8.0%).

Among 150 patients, radical hysterectomy was the most frequent procedure (38.7%), followed by simple hysterectomy (13.3%) and ultra-radical multi-organ surgeries (16.0%) (Table 3). Conservative excisions (LEEP, LLETZ, polypectomy) were performed in 7 cases (4.7%), and fertility-sparing radical trachelectomy in 4 (2.7%). Other mixed or aborted procedures accounted for 10.7%, while unspecified surgeries and no surgery were reported in 10 and 11 patients, respectively.Table 3
Treatment modalities, surgical interventions in patients with GASTable 3 dummy alt text

BO, Bilateral oophorectomy; BSO, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy; CT, chemotherapy; CCRT, concomitant chemoradiotherapy; EL-BO, Exploratory laparotomy-BO; IO, Immunotherapy; PLND, pelvic lymphadenectomy; RT, Radiotherapy; TCM, Traditional Chinese medicine.Tjokroprawiro. Clinicopathological characteristics and outcomes of gastric-type adenocarcinoma of the cervix. AJOG Glob Rep 2026.

Among non-surgical treatments, CCRT was the most frequently reported approach (34.7%). Chemotherapy (CT) alone was described in 14% cases, followed by CT + RT (8.7%) and RT alone (6.7%). Only a small number of patients received multimodal regimens such as CT + RT + TT, CT + TT, or CCRT combined with IO, each accounting for fewer than 2% of cases. TCM was reported in one patient (0.7%). Notably, 35 patients (23.3%) received no non-surgical treatment, while details were unavailable for 12 patients (8.0%).

For recurrence treatment, the majority of cases lacked reported data (68.7%). Among those with available information, 22% was not treated or had no recurrence. TT was the most frequently administered modality for recurrent disease (3.3%), followed by CCRT, IO, and combinations of CT, TT, IO, and RT, each reported in isolated cases. Surgical intervention was only reported in one patient undergoing EL-BO and another treated with CCRT + RT combined with lung segmentectomy.

Across reported cases of gastric-type adenocarcinoma, IHC profiling most consistently revealed diffuse CK7 expression together with frequent positivity for MUC6 and PAX8, while p16 staining was predominantly negative or non-block, and ER/PR were rarely expressed; importantly, abnormal p53 expression, reflecting mutant patterns, was observed in the majority of tumors, underscoring its diagnostic relevance (Table 4). Additional markers such as CEA and CDX2 showed variable expression across studies. In terms of circulating biomarkers, CA19-9 was elevated in most tested patients, CA-125 was increased in a notable proportion, whereas CEA was less frequently raised, indicating a heterogeneous but clinically relevant pattern of serum tumor marker elevation.Table 4

For serum tumor markers, positive = elevated, negative = normal; IHC, Immunohistochemistry; N/A, Not available data.Tjokroprawiro. Clinicopathological characteristics and outcomes of gastric-type adenocarcinoma of the cervix. AJOG Glob Rep 2026.

A total of 35 patients (23.3%) were alive with no evidence of disease (ANED), 42 patients (28.0%) were alive with disease (AWD), and 22 patients (14.7%) had died of disease (DOD) at last follow-up (Table 5). Survival status was not reported in 44 patients (29.3%). Overall, 77 of 150 patients (51.3%) were alive at last follow-up.Table 5
Treatment outcomes, recurrence, site of recurrence and survival rates in patients with GASTable 5 dummy alt text

Time to recurrence not reported/total recurrence15/4334.9
No recurrence/total available data49/9253.3
Site of recurrence
Local (pelvic/cervix/vagina)5/4311.6
Regional (pelvic LN, para-aortic LN)2/434.6
Distant (lung, liver, bone, peritoneum, etc.)14/4332.6

Survival
6 mo survival, alive/total available data72/7793.5

5-y survival, alive/total available data7/2626.9Tjokroprawiro. Clinicopathological characteristics and outcomes of gastric-type adenocarcinoma of the cervix. AJOG Glob Rep 2026.

Recurrrence data were only available in 92 patients (61.3%). Recurrence occurred in 43 of 92 patients (46.7%). Among patients with documented recurrence (n=43), 14 patients (32.6%) recurred within 1 year, 11 (25.6%) within 1 to 3 years, and 3 (7.0%) after more than 3 years, while timing was not reported in 15 patients (34.9%). Among cases with specified recurrence sites, distant metastases were most frequent (14/43, 32.6%), followed by local recurrence involving the pelvis, cervix, or vagina (5/43, 11.6%), and regional lymph node involvement (2/43, 4.6%). The site of recurrence was not specified in 22 patients (51.2%).

Survival analysis based on available follow-up data demonstrated survival rates of 93.5% (72/77) at 6 months, 87.1% (54/62) at 1 year, 68.9% (31/45) at 2 years, 48.5% (16/33) at 3 years, and 26.9% (7/26) at 5 years.

Among 150 patients, recurrence status was reported in 92 cases, with an overall recurrence rate of 46.7% (43/92). Recurrence frequency increased with advancing FIGO stage, occurring in 11.8% of stage I cases (4/34), 36.4% of stage II cases (12/33), 35.7% of stage III cases (10/28), and 50.0% of stage IV cases (12/24). Across treatment categories, recurrence was observed in all patients managed with non-surgical therapy alone, whereas lower recurrence rates were noted in patients treated with surgery only or surgery with adjuvant therapy, although recurrence data were incomplete in a substantial proportion of cases across all stages. Missing recurrence information was most frequent in advanced-stage disease and in patients receiving multimodal treatment (Supplementary Table 6).

Recurrence data were available for 92 patients. Overall, recurrence was observed in 43/92 patients (46.7%). When stratified by treatment category, recurrence occurred in all patients treated with non-surgical modalities (9/9, 100%), compared with 6 of 25 patients (24.0%) treated with surgery alone and 28 of 58 patients (48.3%) treated with surgery plus adjuvant therapy. Exploratory comparison using Fisher’s exact test demonstrated a significant difference in recurrence proportions across treatment groups (P<.001) (Supplementary Table 7).

The distribution of FIGO stage differed across treatment categories (Supplementary Table 8). Among patients managed without surgery (n=7), no cases were diagnosed at FIGO stage I, while the majority presented with advanced disease, including stage III (14.3%) and stage IV (30.8%). In the surgery-only group (n=21), early-stage disease predominated, with FIGO stage I accounting for 45.0% of cases, whereas stage III and IV disease were infrequent, each representing 7.1% and 7.7% of patients, respectively. Patients treated with surgery followed by adjuvant therapy (n=49) exhibited a wider distribution of disease stages. This group included 55.0% of all FIGO stage I patients, as well as the majority of patients with stage II (77.8%), stage III (78.6%), and stage IV disease (61.5%). A notable proportion of patients with undetermined stage also received adjuvant therapy (41.7%). Overall, treatment allocation varied according to disease stage, with non-surgical management largely applied in advanced-stage disease and adjuvant therapy commonly administered across intermediate and advanced FIGO stages.

Kaplan-Meier survival analysis was performed using reconstructed individual patient-level data from 77 patients with available follow-up information to estimate overall survival. For stage-stratified analysis, 12 patients with carcinoma in situ or undetermined FIGO stage were excluded, leaving 65 patients for comparison across FIGO stages. Survival distributions differed significantly by FIGO stage based on the log-rank test (log-rank P=5.14 × 10⁻⁷, P<.001). Median overall survival for the entire cohort was 82 months (min-max, 2–94 months). Stage-specific median survival was longest in FIGO stage I at 94 months (min-max, 6–94 months), followed by stage II at 38 months (min-max, 2–82 months), and shortest in stage IV at 21.5 months (min-max, 3–36 months). Median survival was not reached in FIGO stage III due to a limited number of death events and substantial censoring during follow-up (Figure 2, A).Figure 2
Pooled Kaplan-Meier survival curves for OS in patients of different FIGO stages and treatmentFigure 2 dummy alt textTjokroprawiro. Clinicopathological characteristics and outcomes of gastric-type adenocarcinoma of the cervix. AJOG Glob Rep 2026.

Kaplan-Meier survival analysis was performed using reconstructed individual patient-level data from 77 patients with available follow-up information to evaluate overall survival according to treatment modality. Patients were categorized into surgery only (n=21), surgery with adjuvant therapy (n=49), and non-surgical management (n=7). Survival distributions differed significantly across treatment groups based on the log-rank test (log-rank P=4.41 × 10⁻⁵, P<.001). Median overall survival was longest among patients treated with surgery alone at 82 months (min-max, 4–94 months), followed by those receiving surgery with adjuvant therapy at 65 months (min-max, 2–86 months), whereas patients managed without surgery had the poorest outcome, with a median survival of 7 months (min-max, 3–24.2 months) (Figure 2, B).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Until the late 2000s, cervical adenocarcinoma was largely regarded by gynecological pathologists and oncologists as a single HPV-driven disease featuring its aetiology, pathogenesis, and biology. This view has since shifted, as endocervical adenocarcinoma is now recognized as a heterogeneous spectrum of tumors classified according to their HPV association status. The 2020 WHO classification distinguishes cervical adenocarcinomas into HPV-associated and HPV-independent groups. GAS of the cervix represents a distinctive and uncommon histologic subtype of cervical adenocarcinoma that has only recently gained recognition as an HPV-independent malignancy.4,15

sec.title: Novel contributions of the present review
sec.p: Several narrative and cohort-based studies have previously described gastric-type adenocarcinoma of the cervix, focusing on histopathologic classification, molecular characteristics, and institutional treatment experience. Mikami (2020) and Nishio et al. (2024) provided comprehensive narrative overviews emphasizing diagnostic criteria and molecular pathways, whereas Arango-Bravo et al. (2024) discussed GAS within the broader context of rare CC histologies. While these contributions are foundational, they do not systematically synthesize patient-level clinical trajectories or outcomes across heterogeneous settings.3,7,16

In contrast, the present study represents the first systematic review integrating individual patient data from published case reports and case series across multiple continents. By aggregating 150 patients from 59 studies, this review captures the full clinical spectrum of GAS, including early-stage incidental disease, locally advanced presentations, and disseminated cases that are typically underrepresented in single-center cohorts.

Importantly, large retrospective series such as the cohort reported by Ehmann et al.6 provide valuable survival benchmarks within high-resource tertiary centers, but their findings may not fully reflect global practice patterns. Our systematic synthesis complements these data by incorporating diverse diagnostic pathways, treatment strategies, and follow-up durations, thereby highlighting real-world variability and recurrent patterns of failure. By pooling available follow-up information, we further extend prior literature through descriptive Kaplan-Meier survival estimates stratified by FIGO stage and treatment, enabling comparative interpretation across disease stages despite the inherent limitations of case-based evidence. This approach offers a unique perspective on outcome heterogeneity and underscores gaps that cannot be addressed by narrative reviews or single-institution cohorts alone.

sec.title: Trends and challenges of diagnosing GAS
sec.p: GAS represents over 10% of cervical adenocarcinomas worldwide. As case recognition has increased, it has been classified into subtypes, including poorly differentiated forms and well-differentiated variants, the latter also known as mucinous adenocarcinoma, adenoma malignum, or MDA. The term “gastric” reflects its morphological resemblance to gastric and pancreatobiliary tissue lining.17 A subset of atypical LEGH and gastric-type adenocarcinoma in situ (GAIS) has been linked to, and may represent, precursor lesions of GAS.18 This association is further supported by molecular studies demonstrating GNAS, KRAS, and STK11 mutations in LEGH, suggesting its potential role in the early development of GAS.19,20 In contrast to usual cervical adenocarcinoma, GAS usually develops in the upper cervical canal and presents as a bulky cervical mass that expands axially toward the lower uterus and ectocervix. Recognition of this variant is essential for pathologists due to its distinctive clinical profile.21,22

Accurate diagnosis of GAS remains the greatest obstacle in clinical practice, with reported misdiagnosis rates approaching as high as 34%,23 leading to frequent misdiagnosis and delayed treatment. The challenge is particularly evident when the tumor extends into the uterine corpus or adnexa, or when a precursor lesion is not clearly identified.24 Consequently, GAS have at times been initially misdiagnosed as endometrial or ovarian carcinoma, as metastatic disease from an extragenital site, or have been mistyped as usual-type adenocarcinoma.25 Its deceptively benign histologic appearance contrasts with its highly aggressive biological behavior. Moreover, the bland cytology, deep endocervical location, and HPV-independent biology of GAS markedly reduce the sensitivity of routine screening methods such as Pap smears and HPV testing. Standard diagnostic procedures such as colposcopy-guided biopsy, endocervical curettage, and cervical scraping also frequently fail to detect the lesion.9,26,27

The most common initial presentations were vaginal bleeding and vaginal discharge. A few patients in our review had a comorbid condition of PJS, a condition known to confer a markedly increased lifetime risk of gastrointestinal and extra-gastrointestinal malignancies, including cancer of the stomach (29%), colon (39%), breast (54%), lung (15%), uterus (9%), and ovary (21%). If individuals diagnosed with PJS report abnormal gynaecological symptoms such as abdominal pain, vaginal discharge, or vaginal bleeding, a comprehensive gynaecological examination and monitoring should be undertaken to ensure early detection of CC.28

Given the indolent presentation of GAS, recognition of this tumor through imaging is essential to prevent diagnostic delay. USG, CT, and MRI have all been utilized both for diagnostic assessment and for perioperative planning. USG is commonly used as the initial imaging modality for suspected endocervical pathology and may demonstrate cervical hypertrophy or cystic changes in GAS. Nonetheless, USG has limited sensitivity in detecting multicystic lesions. Some studies reported recognition rates of around 47% to 50% . More detailed evaluation has shown that GAS lesions on TVUSG may appear multilocular or solid, often with smooth margins and multiple small locules ranging up to 20 locules (<1 cm each), while Doppler can reveal increased vascularity. In certain cases, diffuse cervical infiltration may also be visualized, raising suspicion of malignancy.29

CT is not routinely employed for the evaluation of GAS, but certain features may be observed. Cervical hypertrophy is more likely to appear in advanced disease, while early-stage cases may show no obvious enlargement. Non-contrast CT can demonstrate low-density, non-enhancing areas, whereas contrast-enhanced scans may reveal multicystic lesions with enhancing low-density components. PET-CT typically shows heterogeneous, moderate fluorodeoxyglucose uptake that is suggestive of malignancy.21,30,31

Among imaging modalities, MRI is regarded as the preferred imaging modality for the early evaluation of GAS since it offers superior soft-tissue contrast, enables assessment of tumor margins, and can delineate local extension more accurately than ultrasound or CT. It provides correlation with pathological findings and can assess advanced disease by identifying solid components and tumor extension, thereby assisting surgical planning. Characteristic MRI features of GAS include exophytic or endophytic masses with ill-defined margins, intratumoral cysts, and a tendency to involve the upper cervical canal or the entire cervix. On imaging, lesions typically appear hypointense on T1-weighted images and hyperintense on T2-weighted images.32,33 Furthermore, this malignancy has been described as a “cosmic model”: the lesion frequently appears higher on the cervix, and there are some smaller cysts or solid substances in the center of the lesion, surrounded by relatively large cysts.26 Nevertheless, it should be emphasized that imaging alone cannot establish a definitive diagnosis, as the gold standard for GAS remains histopathological examination.

This morphology malignancy is characterized by tumor cells exhibiting gastric-like appearance differentiation with three main diagnostic features marked by abundant clear or pale eosinophilic cytoplasm, well-defined cytoplasmic borders, a low nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio, and nuclei located at the base. The cytological findings in well-differentiated GAS are characterized by monolayered or honeycombed cells with extensive vacuolated foamy cytoplasm, infrequent goblet cells, and minimal to no activity in mitosis. In contrast, poorly differentiated GAS are distinguished by dense 3-dimensional clusters or pseudostratified tumor cells with hyperchromatic nuclei, uneven nuclear membranes, coarse chromatin, distinct nucleoli, and signals of mitotic activity or focal apoptosis.34

MDA, which is part of the GAS spectrum, has a low-grade morphology with well-differentiated glands. Tumors were classified as MDA when the bulk of tumor in the cervix (≥90%) was cytologically low grade (minimal nuclear atypia, abundant apical mucin) with well-formed glands sometimes showing the classically described “claw-like” growth pattern and some stromal desmoplasia. MDA tends to have nuclei with an irregular contour, hyperchromasia, loss of polarity but without intranuclear cytoplasmic inclusion.35

sec.title: Diagnostic markers in GAS
sec.p: Neutral mucins in the cytoplasm stain pale pinkish-red on the Alcian blue/periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) special staining as opposed to the deep purple of acid mucins in the normal endocervix . The term gastric-type adenocarcinoma was introduced because of the morphological features and the expression Gastric-type mucin markers, such as MUC6 or HIK1083. The MUC6 antibody recognises pyloric gland mucin.36, 37, 38 In line with previous reports, our review demonstrated MUC6 positivity in 79 of 80 patients (98.8%), Its high expression in GAS reflects the underlying gastric differentiation of these tumors, thereby supporting its role in distinguishing GAS from other mucinous or HPV-associated cervical neoplasms.39, 40, 41 Subsequently, previously study on HIK1083 monoclonal antibody has been proven to be valuable in diagnosis of MDA.42

Because GAS is morphologically similar to pancreatic and biliary tract adenocarcinomas and shares a comparable immunohistochemical profile, additional markers are required for accurate diagnosis. PAX8, a transcription factor critical for the embryogenesis of the thyroid gland, kidney, and Müllerian system, is normally expressed in these epithelia as well as in most carcinomas arising from them. It has emerged as a novel marker for differentiating tumor types, particularly in the context of ovarian epithelial carcinomas.43,44

A distinguishing feature is that the majority of the cases demonstrate positive PAX8 expression, either diffusely or focally. Our review found a high prevalence of PAX8 expression, with positive rate of 93.6% among 31 patients reported in 18 articles, which allows for differ these malignancies from adenocarcinomas of gastrointestinal, pancreatic, or biliary origin. In addition, PAX8 represents a valuable marker in differentiating GAS from other mucinous adenocarcinomas that are generally negative.45,46 However, PAX2 expression is typically negative for GAS and therefore not commonly tested. Among our 150 patients, only one underwent PAX2 testing, which showed a positive result. Notably, PAX2 positivity is more often associated with LEGH as a recognized precursor lesion rather than with GAS itself.47 p16 is a cyclin-dependent kinase-4 inhibitor that becomes overexpressed in the presence of integrated oncogenic HPV. This marker is typically negative or only focally positive in GAS, while diffuse positivity is seen in HPV-associated usual-type endocervical adenocarcinoma. Our study demonstrated p16 positivity corresponding to an overall expression rate of 81.7%. Most usual HPV-related cervical adenocarcinomas look morphologically quite different from GAS in that they are not overtly mucinous and they exhibit much more conspicuous apoptotic activity. However, in problematic cases, p16 may be useful in distinguishing GAS (negative or focally positive) from an HPV related adenocarcinoma (diffuse “block-type” immunoreactivity). Nonetheless, p16 overexpression may occasionally occur in GAS independent of HPV infection, highlighting the importance of confirmatory HPV testing when diffuse p16 expression is observed.48,49

Mutant p53 immunostaining is observed in roughly half of GAS cases. This strong positive or abnormal expression reflects underlying oncogenic activity and is thought to play a central role in tumor development, while also contributing to resistance to RT and CT.26,50 A low Ki- 67 proliferation index, usually <40%, is one of the classical immune markers of gastric-type mucus, leading to the illusion that the tumor’s biological activity is relatively benign despite the tumor’s aggressive behavior.51

Moreover, estrogen and progesterone receptors (ER and PR) in GAS are consistently negative, suggesting that tumor development is independent of hormonal influence.37 CEA and CK7 are generally positive, while CK20 and CDX2 show variable expression, with up to half of cases demonstrating positivity. CA19-9 is elevated in over half of GAS patients, while approximately one-third demonstrate increased CA125 levels; notably, CA125 elevation is often associated with pelvic or peritoneal metastasis.23,52

Molecular profiling has highlighted distinct genetic alterations in GAS compared with HPV-associated adenocarcinomas. Mutations in TP53, STK11, SMAD4, and HLA-B occur more frequently in GAS, whereas alterations in KRAS, GNAS, and CDKN2A are often associated with adverse outcomes irrespective of histological subtype. The most affected pathways involve signal transduction, DNA damage repair, and epithelial-mesenchymal transition.53

The predominance of studies originated from Asia, particularly China, Japan, and South Korea, might likely reflect diagnostic and referral patterns rather than a true geographic predilection. Pathologists in these countries were among the first to characterize GAS, leading to greater awareness and more frequent use of ancillary tools such as IHC and advanced imaging, which facilitate recognition of this entity. High-volume tertiary centers and a strong publication culture further contribute to the number of cases reported from these regions. In comparison, underdiagnosis may exist in regions with limited pathology infrastructure, emphasizing the critical need for enhanced identification and diagnostic availability in low-resource settings.

MDA has been linked to PJS through germline and somatic mutations in the STK11/LKB1 tumor suppressor gene. Somatic STK11 mutations have been confirmed in more than half of mucinous MDAs, and their presence has been correlated with poorer oncologic outcomes. These findings emphasize the importance of molecular characterization in refining diagnosis, prognostication, and potentially identifying targets for therapy in the GAS spectrum.38,54

sec.title: Proposed practical diagnostic and treatment algorithm
sec.p:
To enhance clinical applicability, we propose a pragmatic, stepwise diagnostic algorithm for GAS that integrates clinical assessment, cytology, imaging, histopathology, and targeted IHC, while remaining adaptable to low-resource settings with limited diagnostic capabilities (Figure 3). Given the HPV-independent nature of GAS, the algorithm emphasizes that negative cytology or HPV testing does not exclude malignancy in clinically suspicious cases and should prompt further imaging and tissue-based evaluation. p16 IHC serves as an accessible triage tool to distinguish HPV-associated from HPV-independent adenocarcinomas, followed by selective use of gastric markers such as HIK1083 and MUC6, together with ER and PR, to support the diagnosis of GAS and exclude histologic mimics. Imaging is structured hierarchically, prioritizing transvaginal ultrasound or CT when MRI is unavailable, with advanced modalities reserved for staging when resources allow. Importantly, treatment selection and optimization of therapeutic course and outcomes should be determined through multidisciplinary tumor board (MTB) discussion, reflecting disease stage, resectability, and available systemic options, thereby aligning diagnostic findings with individualized management in real-world practice.Figure 3
Proposed pragmatic diagnostic algorithm for GAS, based on literature synthesis and expert interpretationFigure 3 dummy alt textTjokroprawiro. Clinicopathological characteristics and outcomes of gastric-type adenocarcinoma of the cervix. AJOG Glob Rep 2026.

A proposed pragmatic treatment decision framework for GAS is presented in Figure 4. This framework is based on synthesis of available literature, reported clinicopathologic patterns, and expert interpretation, and should be regarded as a conceptual guide rather than a definitive evidence-based standard. For pre-invasive gastric-type lesions, including LEGH and gAIS, diagnosis should be confirmed by histopathology with supportive IHC demonstrating gastric differentiation (MUC6 and/or HIK1083 positivity, HPV negativity, and negative or patchy p16 expression). Given the risk of occult invasion and progression, hysterectomy is generally considered appropriate, while fertility-sparing procedures may be cautiously considered only in highly selected patients with negative margins and close surveillance.Figure 4
Proposed pragmatic treatment decision framework for GAS, based on available evidence and expert-informed clinical reasoningFigure 4 dummy alt textTjokroprawiro. Clinicopathological characteristics and outcomes of gastric-type adenocarcinoma of the cervix. AJOG Glob Rep 2026.

In early-stage invasive GAS (FIGO IA1-IB2 and selected IIA1), radical hysterectomy with pelvic lymph node assessment is commonly employed, with adjuvant RT and concurrent platinum-based CT considered in the presence of established high-risk pathologic features. For locally advanced disease (FIGO IIB-IVA), CCRT remains the principal treatment modality where available, although alternative strategies, including surgery in carefully selected cases or limited-resource settings, may be considered. In metastatic or recurrent disease, systemic therapy represents the primary approach, with salvage surgery, RT, or biomarker-directed therapies considered based on disease extent, prior treatment, and patient fitness.

Given the rarity, aggressive behavior, and limited GAS-specific evidence, multidisciplinary tumor board discussion remains essential. This proposed framework is intended to support structured, individualized clinical decision-making while acknowledging the need for further evidence to establish standardized management strategies (Figure 4).

sec.title: Management of GAS and potential targeted therapies
sec.p: Given the rarity of GAS and the scarcity of robust evidence on treatment outcomes, the optimal treatment approach for GAS remains undefined. Surgical excision of the uterus, adnexa, omentum, appendix, and macroscopic tumor may be suggested considering the possibility to spread across surfaces into the peritoneal cavity and the greater likelihood of developing at an advanced stage.54 Since the biological behavior of GAS resembles the characteristics of small-cell neuroendocrine carcinoma, the 2021 Chinese Expert Consensus suggested managing these tumors in accordance with the NCCN guidelines for cervical small-cell neuroendocrine carcinoma.23

According to current guidelines, the preferred surgical approach for patients with GAS ≤4 cm is primary radical hysterectomy with pelvic lymphadenectomy, with or without para-aortic lymph node sampling. In view of the high invasiveness of GAS, fertility-sparing procedures and ovarian preservation are not recommended. In selected stage I patients, NCCN also stated that sentinel lymph node mapping is part of the standard of care. The Chinese expert consensus additionally advises removal of the bilateral adnexa, greater omentum, appendix, and any metastatic lesions within the pelvic or abdominal cavity.55

According to the NCCN, enrollment in clinical trials remains essential to explore novel strategies such as combining IO with CT, RT, or anti-angiogenic agents. In routine practice, risk stratification for postoperative adjuvant therapy still follows Sedlis and Peters’ criteria. High-risk patients, defined by factors such as positive lymph nodes, positive surgical margins, or parametrial invasion, are recommended to receive external beam RT with or without concurrent platinum-based CT. Adenocarcinomas, along with large tumor size (>4 cm) and LVSI, are associated with higher recurrence.56 These findings support the use of adjuvant therapy in selected patients, although evidence specific to GAS remains limited. They also highlight the varying recurrence patterns, emphasizing that adjuvant treatment should be individualized.

A prospective study by the Japanese Clinical Oncology Group demonstrated significantly worse survival and more frequent metastasis in patients with stage I-II GAS treated with radical hysterectomy and tailored radiation compared with other histologies. The effectiveness of CT remains uncertain, as GAS exhibits relative chemoresistance; fewer than half of patients respond to platinum-taxane combinations and well-differentiated tumors in particular show poor responsiveness to standard chemoradiation.57,58 These observations raise concern over the applicability of existing treatment protocols and underscore the urgent need for GAS-specific therapeutic strategies. Recent clinical trials have evaluated the efficacy and safety of pembrolizumab in CC. These studies demonstrated benefit when pembrolizumab was added to cisplatin-paclitaxel as first-line therapy for PD-L1-positive recurrent or metastatic disease.59,60 Although data specific to GAS are lacking, these findings suggest the potential therapeutic value of PD-1/PD-L1-targeted approaches. Further investigation, including trials combining IO with radiation in the recurrent setting, may help clarify the role of immune checkpoint inhibitors for rare histological subtypes such as GAS.

Given the aggressive nature of GAS and its resistance to conventional CT, new targeted therapies such as PARP inhibitorsare being explored to improve treatment outcomes.61 Although these agents have shown promise in other cancers with DNA repair deficiencies, clinical evidence in GAS remains extremely limited. To date, only a single published case describes a BRCA2-mutated advanced gastric-type endocervical adenocarcinoma patient treated with olaparib maintenance therapy, in which tumor growth was stabilized. While this suggests potential clinical benefit from mutation-driven PARP inhibition, the evidence is anecdotal, and larger studies are needed to confirm efficacy and define the role of PARP inhibitors in GAS.62

Similarly, HER2-targeted therapy, well established in other solid tumors, is still largely theoretical in GAS due to limited routine HER2 testing and scarce clinical data; however, several observational studies have reported HER2 amplification in a meaningful subset of cases. For example, HER2 amplification was found in about 15.5% of GAS cases in a cohort of 58 patients (with approximately 17.2% overexpression by IHC) and was associated with worse progression-free survival, while earlier series showed around 14.7% amplification in another GAS cohort, suggesting a real biological signal rather than random noise.52,63,64 These data indicate that HER2 amplification does occur in a non-negligible fraction of GAS and could, especially with HER2-directed ADCs that deliver cytotoxic agents directly to HER2-overexpressing cells, provide a promising avenue for future targeted therapy.65

In recurrent or metastatic HPV-independent GAS, treatment options remain extremely limited. Recent real-world evidence highlights the potential of antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs) in this setting. In a small Singapore case series, one patient with metastatic GAS treated with HER2-directed ADCs achieved limited efficacy but tolerated therapy without new safety signals.66 Complementing this, Babayan et al. reported a 47-year-old patient with stage IV GAS who progressed after surgery, CT, RT, and bevacizumab, and subsequently received tisotumab vedotin (TV).67 After four cycles, the patient achieved a complete response, which persisted for at least 8 months at the time of reporting. The main adverse effects were ocular toxicities, including dry eye, punctate epithelial keratitis, and pseudomembranes, requiring dose reductions and intensive ophthalmologic interventions, while non-ocular toxicities were mild (grade 1 nausea and emesis). These cases collectively suggest that ADCs, including TV, can elicit meaningful responses in HPV-independent GAS, but highlight the need for careful toxicity management and further studies to confirm efficacy and safety in this rare subtype.

Following definitive treatment for disease confined to the cervix, NCCN guidelines recommend structured surveillance: every 3 to 6 months during the first 2 years, every 6 to 12 months for years three to five, and annually thereafter, tailored to recurrence risk. Imaging is performed based on disease stage and the presence of symptoms.53 While the scarcity of data poses challenges, following established guidelines provides a valuable framework for managing GAS.

sec.title: Outcomes and prognosis of GAS
sec.p: GAS is associated with an aggressive disease course by its clinical presentation, often marked by deep stromal invasion, lymphovascular space invasion (LVSI), and atypical metastatic pattern. Previous reported metastatic sites include the omentum, liver, brain, and bone, along with an increased risk of distant metastases, particularly to the ovaries, peritoneum, and abdominal lymph nodes, which are rarely affected in conventional endocervical adenocarcinoma.68,69 Our study demonstrated that recurrence occurred in over one quarter of patients. Of these, 32.6% recurred within 1 year, 25.6% within 1 to 3 years, and 7% after more than 3 years, while timing was not reported in 44.2% of cases. Among documented sites of recurrence, distant metastases were most frequent, followed by local recurrence in the pelvis, cervix, or vagina and regional lymph node involvement.

In the present systematic synthesis, survival outcomes were further explored using Kaplan-Meier analysis based on reconstructed individual patient-level data, providing a complementary perspective to descriptive survival proportions reported in prior studies. Stage-stratified analysis demonstrated a clear separation of survival curves across FIGO stages, with a significant stage-dependent decline in overall survival. Median survival was longest in FIGO stage I and progressively shortened with advancing stage. These findings reinforce FIGO stage as the dominant prognostic determinant in gastric-type adenocarcinoma of the cervix and are consistent with prior institutional cohort data demonstrating inferior outcomes across all stages compared with HPV-associated adenocarcinoma, including early-stage disease.6

When outcomes were examined by treatment modality, patients managed with surgery alone showed longer median overall survival than those receiving surgery with adjuvant therapy, whereas non-surgically treated patients experienced the poorest survival. However, supplementary stage-stratified analyses revealed that treatment allocation closely mirrored disease stage. Surgery-only approaches were predominantly applied in early-stage disease, while combined or non-surgical treatments were more frequently used in locally advanced or metastatic cases. Similarly, recurrence rates increased with advancing stage regardless of treatment modality. Collectively, these observations indicate that apparent survival differences across treatment groups are largely driven by underlying stage distribution rather than intrinsic treatment efficacy, underscoring the limitations of case-based evidence in defining optimal stage-specific management strategies.

Moreover, previous evidence indicates that this unfavourable overall prognosis is evident across all stages, as patients with GAS experience worse survival even when diagnosed at an early stage compared to other cervical adenocarcinoma subtypes,16,70,71 with a 2-year survival of just 20% to 30% across all stages and only around 50% for stage I patients.22 In contrast, our review demonstrated more favourable survival, with 6-month, 1-year, and 2-year survival rates of 93.5%, 87.1%, and 68.9%, respectively. Several factors may explain this discrepancy. First, the evidence synthesized in our review was largely derived from case reports and small series, which are inherently prone to reporting and selection bias.

On the other hand, a retrospective study demonstrated markedly poorer long-term outcomes in GAS, with 5- and 10-year disease-specific survival of 42% and 31%, compared with 91% and 81% in usual HPV-associated endocervical adenocarcinoma.70 Our review shows a similar pattern that long-term outcome declined substantially, with 3-year and 5-year survival of only 48.5% and 26.9%, respectively. Because of its propensity for neurovascular invasion, frequent p53 mutations, and resistance to CT and RT, the prognosis of GAS is poorer than that of usual-type endocervical adenocarcinoma or squamous cell carcinoma.

These findings underscore the critical importance of early detection, which, although rarely achieved, may offer a survival advantage when diagnosis is made at an earlier stage. Aside from that, while our review suggests a more favourable early survival rate than previously described, long-term outcomes for GAS remain poor and should be interpreted with caution. It should be noted, however, that survival status was not reported in 44 patients (29.3%), introducing potential bias and limiting the precision of these estimates. Thus, larger prospective cohorts with standardized follow-up are needed to clarify the true prognosis of this rare and aggressive malignancy.

sec.title: Clinical implications, research gaps, and future directions
sec.p: Despite increasing recognition of GAS, major gaps remain. No specific treatment guidelines exist, and current management is still extrapolated from other CC subtypes. The safety of fertility-sparing approaches has not been established, and systemic therapy options remain limited due to chemoresistance and the absence of validated targeted or immune-based strategies. Although recurrent molecular alterations such as TP53 and STK11 have been identified, their clinical implications are poorly defined. Furthermore, most available data come from high-resource settings where access to IHC and advanced pathology is routine, raising concern for an iceberg phenomenon in low-resource regions where limited diagnostic capacity likely leads to under-recognition and misdiagnosis. Collectively, these pitfalls underscore the urgent need for multicenter prospective studies and global efforts to refine diagnostic accuracy and establish evidence-based management strategies for this rare but aggressive malignancy. In this context, we propose pragmatic, expert-informed diagnostic and treatment frameworks to support structured clinical decision-making while acknowledging current evidence limitations.

sec.title: Strength and limitation
sec.p: This study represents the first comprehensive systematic review of case reports and small series on gastric-type adenocarcinoma of the cervix, encompassing 59 studies with 150 patients from 15 countries. By consolidating fragmented evidence, it provides an updated overview of clinicopathological characteristics, recurrence patterns, and survival outcomes. The review also draws attention to diagnostic challenges and pitfalls in histopathology, immunohistochemistry, and screening modalities, offering insights that may support earlier recognition and improved management of this rare and aggressive tumor.

However, this review is subject to limitations inherent to case report-based evidence. Reporting of clinical variables was heterogeneous, with frequent missing data on symptoms, imaging characteristics, comorbidities, and longitudinal follow-up. In addition, the predominance of cases originating from Asian tertiary referral centers likely reflects differential diagnostic awareness, access to specialized pathology and IHC, and publication practices rather than true geographic variation. These factors introduce potential publication and referral bias and may limit the generalizability of pooled estimates. Nevertheless, inclusion of cases from multiple countries and healthcare settings provides a broad overview of real-world presentations and outcomes, highlighting gaps that warrant further prospective investigation.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: GAS of the cervix is a rare but highly aggressive HPV-independent malignancy characterized by distinctive morphology, frequent diagnostic pitfalls, limited responsiveness to conventional therapies, and poor long-term survival. Evidence synthesized from 59 case reports and series underscores the urgent need for earlier recognition through improved diagnostic algorithms incorporating IHC and molecular profiling, as well as for the development of tailored therapeutic strategies beyond current HPV-driven CC protocols. Multicenter collaborative studies and prospective registries are essential to refine prognostication, establish evidence-based management, and explore the role of novel systemic and fertility-preserving approaches in this challenging disease.

sec.title: Declaration of AI And AI-assisted technologies in the writing process
sec.p: During the preparation of this work, the authors used AI to enhance the literature search as well as to assist in the writing process to improve the readability and language of the work. The technology was not employed to produce insights, to draw conclusions, or to generate recommendations. After using this technology, the authors reviewed and edited the content as needed and take full responsibility for the content of the publication.

sec.title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
sec.p: Brahmana Askandar Tjokroprawiro: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization. Hanif Ardiansyah Sulistya: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Nicholas Wijaya: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Annisa Aghnia Rahma: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Data curation, Conceptualization. Khoirunnisa Novitasari: Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Renata Alya Ulhaq: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Data curation. Setyo Teguh Waluyo: Writing – review & editing. Peng-Hui Wang: Writing – review & editing.

Exploring tisotumab vedotin in recurrent cervical cancer: a case series including an HPV-independent gastric type adenocarcinoma
Clinicopathological features and outcomes in gastric-type of HPV-independent endocervical adenocarcinomas
Clinical and pathological analyses of gastric-type cervical adenocarcinoma and its prognostic relevance
Gastric-type endocervical adenocarcinoma: an aggressive tumor with unusual metastatic patterns and poor prognosis
Gastric morphology and immunophenotype predict poor outcome in mucinous adenocarcinoma of the uterine cervix

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: The authors declare no acknowledgements in this research.

fn.p: Funding: This research did not receive any specific grant from any party.

Declaration of competing interest: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
Supplementary material associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.xagr.2026.100623.
